And the right perspective on things.

Yeah, I would say, you know, the biggest challenge is that in a partnership versus a top-down CEO-driven org, you know, we basically have a number of investors who are kind of running their own business.
And individually, don't, Derek, do you agree when you, I see you smiling.
So, you know, it depends on who the investor is, but generally, most of the thinking and perspective lies in their head, is locked in their brain.

And if you're not, if the marketing team isn't there when the conversations are happening, and usually they're not, because, you know, it might be that Derek says something genius in a meeting with a founder, or, you know, something comes up in the partner meeting, which I am in, you know, if you're not there to capture it and kind of tease it out, it's an opportunity that's missed.
And I think...
think...
The expectation in our industry too, especially in a world where it's really easy to put out content, there's never been more channels and distribution opportunities, and the bar is pretty low.
It's gotten really noisy, so you want to actually deliver something with great value.
You know, at both Bessemer and Menlo, we've worked with different kind of ways to make the process more efficient, and everybody often is like, well, let's just like farm out the writing.
And it turns out when we do that, the investors are generally dissatisfied because they're external, you know, and in order to work really well with a ghostwriter, I think it takes a number of reps.

And it also takes some pretty detailed feedback in the editing cycle, right, where you're like, I'm not crossing out this word because I don't like this word, I'm crossing it out because it changes the meaning of what.
We're saying or something like that.
And most investors, they're just really busy.
They don't want to spend time doing that.

And it's like, oh, they're not dumb.
They're great writers.
They just don't know our industry well.
Yeah, that makes a ton of sense.

Well, we'd love to maybe jump in and just kind of share a little bit of the journey, which I don't know how familiar you are, but also just talk a little bit about kind of how we work with some of our customers and also some of the things we've done, if you think that makes sense.
All right.
Well, I'll share.
I promise I'll do some demos as well, but I'll share just a quick high level just to give you a little bit of context because I think it's super relevant.
So kind of similar to some of you, I've started a career early on in larger enterprises, but also like from day one, I just realized, I think VCs mostly get this.
Most brands don't, which is if you don't actually act and think more like a publisher, you actually can't grow and build distribution and build trust with brands or with your customers.
And so at IBM, I launched the first publication outside of IBM.com called SecuredIntelligence.com.

And actually, the first year, we generated over $17 million in revenue, and we published over 300 pieces of content, really high quality, and it just became the highest ROI thing that the company did for that business unit.
And so much so that it actually lasted 15 years, it just got folded back into IBM.com, but it became the model for how IBM.com changed their model.
Now they're very content first, and you can, it was this guy, Brian, that I worked with, now is head of IBM.com.
And then at HP, I had built this essentially contributor network, which was kind of what you were saying, like, how do I share the best expertise and create content?
And I just went after and started recruiting experts in the first year we published about.
Two million words and grew the site to over a million visitors a month, which was insane, all organic.
And so from there, as I went into startups and was leading marketing and growth for, you know, HashiCorp, Service Titan, and Scale, I just kind of brought that along with me.
And every single job I had, I was building these kind of growth engines that were all based on content.
The difference was, like, along the way, it was very slow, expensive, and hard to pull together because you're, you know, hiring managing editors.
You're pulling together with a strategy.
You're trying to find subject matter experts.
You're doing all these things, right?
And it just takes time.
And so when I was at DeepGram, I kind of took everything I had learned along those years and hire one person.
And that person and me alone were able to publish over 3,000 pages and create a bunch of programmatic landing pages as well as highly quality editorial.
And we 24x our traffic in six months.
And so it was kind of this huge inflection curve.
And then we built...
A newsletter called AIMinds.com, grew that to 270,000 subscribers.
And then from there, I just started doing these workshops, just kind of teaching people how to approach content and building brand and distribution that way.
And that's kind of how GrowthX got started.
And so, but I think like this might not be as relevant to you all, but it is in a lot of ways, right?
It's also like, on top of that, content has always been this atomic unit for marketing and for, you know, how brands communicate and build trust with their audiences.
But also now a lot of the distribution completely shifted, right?
The distribution layer completely shifted.
And so what we're seeing with a lot of customers is they're spending, you know, their audiences are spending 15, 20 minutes on a chat GPT AI answer instead of like clicking through a bunch of links.
And so now more and more, it's about like, how do you understand your audience and from a deeper perspective and how do you systematically build?
The best answers and best resources to all the things your audience is going to care about, right?

Like, and so, like, in some ways, it's kind of like the start of the funnel or is the start is the kind of your entry ticket to build trust with your audiences that you want to build your reputation with, you know?
And again, it goes back to kind of this credible, comprehensive, fresh content.
And so I think, like, one of the reasons we've seen so much traction in our space is just, and this is probably slightly less relevant for a VC, but in some ways, it's like you said, right?
At Bessemer, trying to outsource to an agency or a freelancer, the quality is really bad.
They don't get your business.
You know, it's hard to find talent like you all out there.
You know, the tools, like, don't really do the work for you or actually help you.
They create more work for you.
And then for a lot of brands, they're trying to acquire customers.
know, paid traffic is just getting really hard.

Which was like stitching together all these pieces and making it work.
And instead like saying, hey, what if there's just like one stop shop that was more outcomes based and that brought together the best experts, but also was AI power.
And so that's what we've done, which is like address the, if you will, like what we think is the four layers in order to be successful with content.

Women go through different phases in life, like fertility, pre-menopausal, post-menopausal.

For over a year that are helped to balance women's hormones along those different, you know, stages of their journey, you know, and there's like so much nuance, right?

Like, you can't get it wrong.

Like, all to kind of generate this context.

These executions, if you will, and publishing content for them.

Yeah, exactly.
And so, so that that's true for like most customers as well.
Like they don't want just any sources or, you know, they don't want us to argument the content that we're creating.

A bit of an example here where this assignment is around like magnesium for menopause relief and like a lot of the sources are highly trusted medical sources, you know, and I'll show you some that are also like agentic workflows as well.

And then, you know, in this case, this is like fairly like tailored to each customer where, you know, how do we achieve quality and you have to start and work backwards from what great looks like and then figure out like what are the steps where AI can actually argument that and then where are the places where you really need human judgment.
And an expert judgment along the way, right?
And so in this case, like we're coming up with an outline, enhancing the outline, drafting the content, and even doing things like fact-checking as well.
And where we're fact-checking like against different, we're essentially splitting the content into chunks and then extracting each passage.
And one thing like you'll notice too, as you're doing like research, even using AI, sometimes like there's like three or four orders of magnitude where like it's citing a source that seems reputable.
But then when you click on that source, it's citing another source that's not reputable, that's citing another source and it's misquoted that source.
And then like by the time you go all the way down to the actual source, it's like super outdated or completely wrong or non-existent.
And somewhere along the way, essentially, like you have like the spread of like bad information, right?
And so what this is doing is like researching each passage in parallel and then verifying each one of
And evidence to make sure that based on the deep research, do we have confidence that this is correct or not?
And if not, why is it not correct?
And how can we correct that, essentially, right?
And so, and then, like, there's things like expert review, where we are reverse engineering how their chief medical officer does reviews on this content, right?
Where it might say, like, hey, this statement is oversimplified.
Right, the statement talks about two neurotransmitters that study your mood, and instead it should be, they're involved in mood and sleep regulation.
They're not actually providing this function, right?
And so it's, like, very, like, medical nuance.
And so you can imagine how this is applied to any domain, know, writing about, where the, like, nuances are different, right?
It might not be technical in the same way.
And can I ask, you do that through some sort of search and verification.
It's not that you've trained on a medical-specific LLM or something like that.
Yeah.
Yeah, so the way to think about this, for instance, with the fact-checking, we're using different sources of research.

So XAI, for instance, has access to a lot of high-quality sources, but then we can also point those deep researchers to very specific sources, including our internal knowledge base that we build for those customers.
And so in some cases, for instance, when we're creating content, grounding is their doc site and Shopify's doc site.

So then we can against that and say, hey, every single thing here has to be fact-checked against Webflow's docs as well as Shopify's docs to make sure we're not saying anything that's actually not true.
So it really depends where should we ground it to, you know?
So it's not about training a model.
It's figuring out what's the source of truth.
And if there's no source of truth, then obviously you can't check against it.
And so do I have, like, is there an account manager who's, like, working with the client to understand what these sources are or how-
Yeah, exactly.

So you have a forward deployed expert who's a managing editor background who's like award-winning journalist type of person like Megan did exactly your jobs like when she was a backstage capital, right?

And so these are people that are like working with you, meeting with you weekly, working directly with your experts like Derek, for example, and figuring out like what are ways to kind of augment and like get more and higher quality outputs, you know?
And then all the way to things like, you know, the imagery and anything else, right?

Like, so what we're trying to do is like figure out what your goals are, who is your audience, have a deeper understanding of your audience, craft all this kind of artifacts, and then figure out what are the right workflows that are both experts involved and AI to improve both the speed, quality, and volume of your outputs in order for you to build trust faster with your audiences, right?
And so then like the final output.
can look very, very different from company to company.

And so you don't want to give bad tax to the company to us, right?

Like long-form interview-based content.

And then maybe...
One fun last thing we wanted to show is the, Danny talked a little bit, we quickly spun out an environment.
Right before the call, like an hour before, was just like playing around and see what we could get from the internet.

it's not going to be, like, I think, like, the way we think about this is usually from a context complexity angle, context complexity, And yours would fit on the high end of content complexity, because you're writing reports, you're writing, like, opinion pieces, it's, like, internal takeaways.
That's your usually content that I saw on the website.
Some other folks, like, first round, like, they might have, like, broader, like, glossaries, or they might have, like, common stuff for early stage founders.
They're, like, those are easier to produce.
So, like, I was just thinking, like, how quickly could we get to something?
And I was just, as you were all talking, was kicking off some researchers and things like that.
I sort of added some things to the knowledge base from the articles that Derek wrote.
And then I created a new context artifact.
If you go back, Marcel, if you go back to the way out, like in the, I started creating like the context artifacts with like what the initial research that I ran with our internal researchers.
So the research, like company research first document ran with our researcher.
takes like 15 minutes.
It's essentially like parallelizing a bunch of calls to EXA, which is like our favorite researcher.
But it can be from anything and it could also be from our knowledge base.
So if you have given us like your internal like company history, that would be the ideal source instead of pulling from the internet.
And then the guidelines just generating like what are we writing for?
So just based on the output of the research was more like, oh, they were probably thinking.
Thinking about Founders Series A, Series B, and the different funds that you have, the Anthology Fund would probably be slightly different than the inflection on the later stage folks.
And the other thing is like a writing guideline.
So what are the things we care about?
And the last thing is like a checklist.
Essentially, a to-do list set, we would have an agent check against that.
So like some of the core documents that we write.
And like I said, like this usually takes days to do it properly, and we would have somebody like talking to your team and like really doing it, right?
But then we plug those into a bunch of agents that we have.
Sometimes we might create like custom agents for the clients, so like multiple normal agents we have that we use with many clients.
But at the end, they added like an expert review that was based on comments for the client.
And in New York case, I was just like thinking like some basic topics and seeing like how far we can get.

Yeah, like a lot of this, like there's a lot of calibration that happens, right, like in order to achieve, like, so let's say like you, you want to have a faster time from Derek having a thought or an insight or something to actually publishing a piece of content.

There's a lot of ways to do that versus like if you're ingesting mostly like transcripts and trying to find different angles and then working some of those angles and scaling, right, like so in this case, like this is a agentic workflow that runs through and evaluates the content against your writing guidelines and runs through different loops, you know, and then it keeps looping through until it passes the evaluation for that writing guidelines.

So that's where like, obviously, like if you have that knowledge or no knowledge at all, like ingested, right, like from you and you haven't calibrated on the context and the context is not right, this stuff is not going to be as good, right, like but but once we calibrate that, what we see, we can
Scale quality, like, much, much better.

One challenge within BC, and I'm just wondering how you guys think about this, is, you know, and it might be just the state, like, I've, at both firms, been the first marketing professional to run marketing, so I've replaced an admin who was, you know, overseeing a PR person writing press releases, but it's kind of like nobody knows where the bodies are buried, and we don't have, you know, necessarily, people aren't saving everything to an internal wiki or something like that, so we don't have the same sort of documentation I would expect it, you know, when I was at Electronic Arts, where everything was very, sort of, like, organized.
So how, you know, and thinking, like, what could I feed this beyond stuff that Derek has already published, you know, it might be investment memos, it might be, you know, Diane, and it has all this.
Stuff for fundraising, but those are also probably like, you couldn't find a more sensitive document in the firm.
And so what kind of security and assurance would we have that like, our stuff is walled off versus other firms, et cetera?
Most companies that we work with, right, like they're not giving us their trade secrets, you know, so because this is meant to be inputs to something that's going to go out.
Public, right?
If you give us your trade secrets, like, you know, like, obviously, it's only if you want it to get published out into the world, right?
Like, so normally, like, even for companies that are like, very well established, like Sentinel One, you'd be shocked at how little they actually have organized.
And so a lot of times what we do, we'll do like, deep dives with folks and try to get that knowledge in any way we can.
Obviously, like, transcripts are probably the easiest way to access that just because they've already had some of those.

Meetings, right, but with lack of that, then, like, we can also schedule these deep dives and then be able to kind of get the right questions, and you'd just be surprised of, like, how much over time you can get out of even a few thoughts and angles, and normally, like, we'll start with, like, one thesis or one theme and one set of topics for one audience and maybe work with one subject matter expert, right, like, in your case, let's say, like, let's work with Derek, right, and then, like, spend some time, like, get to quality, get to publishing, and then once we establish that process, then we can kind of, like, expand from there, essentially, like, get to a win faster, you know, get to quality faster, and then scale from there, but then I think, like, there's this kind of, like, really cool motion where once you start and you see, then I think it becomes easier and easier for others to kind of get behind it because the level of effort on their end goes from, like, maybe, you know, five hours of work versus, like, you know, maybe 15 minutes
And then over time, the more context and knowledge you have, the less you need to actually go from like zero to like 95% ready, you know?

And then we also take away a lot of the last mile things, you know, like we take that on so that like the publishing, the graphics, the things like that, fact-checking, the hyperlinking to other pieces of content, all of that.

## Meeting Purpose

[Demonstrate GrowthX's AI-powered content creation platform to Menlo Ventures' marketing team.](https://fathom.video/share/Wc2nk-gz97-_ggfVzKmEVszYdt5cE934?tab=summary&timestamp=3.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX offers an AI-driven content engine that combines expert knowledge, AI workflows, and human oversight to produce high-quality, targeted content at scale](https://fathom.video/share/Wc2nk-gz97-_ggfVzKmEVszYdt5cE934?tab=summary&timestamp=556.0)
  - [The platform addresses common content creation challenges: quality control, subject matter expertise, and adapting to rapidly changing AI/SEO landscapes](https://fathom.video/share/Wc2nk-gz97-_ggfVzKmEVszYdt5cE934?tab=summary&timestamp=556.0)
  - [GrowthX's approach involves an 8-week strategy sprint to align on goals, quality standards, and workflows before full engagement](https://fathom.video/share/Wc2nk-gz97-_ggfVzKmEVszYdt5cE934?tab=summary&timestamp=938.0)
  - [The service is best suited for companies with urgent growth needs, large content catalogs needing refresh, or those starting content strategies from scratch](https://fathom.video/share/Wc2nk-gz97-_ggfVzKmEVszYdt5cE934?tab=summary&timestamp=742.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX Platform Overview

  - [Combines AI workflows with human expertise to create high-quality content](https://fathom.video/share/Wc2nk-gz97-_ggfVzKmEVszYdt5cE934?tab=summary&timestamp=1129.0)
  - [Uses "context engineering" to capture company/industry-specific knowledge](https://fathom.video/share/Wc2nk-gz97-_ggfVzKmEVszYdt5cE934?tab=summary&timestamp=1129.0)
  - [Offers customizable writing guidelines, fact-checking, and expert review processes](https://fathom.video/share/Wc2nk-gz97-_ggfVzKmEVszYdt5cE934?tab=summary&timestamp=1129.0)
  - [Adapts to different content types: informational, comparisons, programmatic pages, refreshes](https://fathom.video/share/Wc2nk-gz97-_ggfVzKmEVszYdt5cE934?tab=summary&timestamp=1129.0)

### Content Strategy and Execution

  - [Focuses on building trust and credibility through thought leadership](https://fathom.video/share/Wc2nk-gz97-_ggfVzKmEVszYdt5cE934?tab=summary&timestamp=267.0)
  - [Emphasizes creating comprehensive, fresh content to improve AI visibility and SEO](https://fathom.video/share/Wc2nk-gz97-_ggfVzKmEVszYdt5cE934?tab=summary&timestamp=755.0)
  - [Utilizes deep research and fact-checking to ensure accuracy and authority](https://fathom.video/share/Wc2nk-gz97-_ggfVzKmEVszYdt5cE934?tab=summary&timestamp=1129.0)
